text.content: MINI PRD: P72 — Adding New Control Valves to Database
Status: Plan
Batch: 7 | Priority: P72 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Basic instructions for adding custom control valves to database. Minimal context on Cv data entry, when custom valves are needed, or validation. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor
Quick how-to guide for custom control valve database additions. Open with the purpose (adding vendor valve data, custom Cv curves), provide clear steps, explain Cv data entry.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (adding vendor control valve data for accurate sizing), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to step-by-step format**: Numbered list (open database editor → select Control Valve database → Add New → enter Cv data → save).
**Add "Required Data Fields" section**: Manufacturer, model, valve type (pressure reducer, flow control, sustainer), Cv values or Cv curve function.
**Add "Cv Data Entry" section**: How to enter Cv curve from vendor datasheet (Cv vs. opening %).
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify valve appears in selection list, test auto-sizing.
**Add internal links**: P43 FluidFlow Database, P86 Hydraulic Components - Controllers.
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
Glossary Links (first use only)
None
Internal Links
UP: P43 FluidFlow Database
SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles
DOWN: P86 Hydraulic Components - Controllers
Selling Message
None
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Control valve database: Default database for several vendors
Cv values/Cv curve function supported
Auto-sizing available
Control valve types: Pressure reducer, Sustainer, Pressure control, Flow control, Gas control
MINI PRD: P73 — Adding New Valves to Database
Status: Plan
Batch: 7 | Priority: P73 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Basic instructions for adding custom valves (butterfly, gate, globe, etc.) to database. Minimal context on K-factor entry, when custom valves are needed, or validation. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor
Quick how-to guide for custom valve database additions. Open with the purpose (adding vendor valve data, custom K-factors), provide clear steps, explain K-factor data entry.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (adding vendor valve data for accurate pressure drop calculations), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to step-by-step format**: Numbered list (open database editor → select Valve database → Add New → enter K-factor data → save).
**Add "Required Data Fields" section**: Manufacturer, model, valve type (butterfly, gate, globe, ball, etc.), K-factor vs. opening %, size.
**Add "K-Factor Data Entry" section**: How to enter K-factor curve from vendor datasheet (K vs. opening %).
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify valve appears in selection list, test in model.
**Add internal links**: P43 FluidFlow Database, P85 Hydraulic Components - Valves.
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
Glossary Links (first use only)
None
Internal Links
UP: P43 FluidFlow Database
SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles
DOWN: P85 Hydraulic Components - Valves
Selling Message
None
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Valve database: 14 types (Butterfly, Gate, Globe, Ball, Diaphragm, Plug, Needle, Y-globe, Slide, Penstock, 3-way, Fire hydrant, Pinch)
Customizable: Users can add valve models
K-factor calculation methods: Idelchik, Miller, Crane, SAE
MINI PRD: P74 — Adding New General Resistances to Database
Status: Plan
Batch: 7 | Priority: P74 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Basic instructions for adding custom general resistances to database. Minimal context on K/Kf/Kv values, when custom resistances are needed, or validation. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor
Quick how-to guide for custom general resistance database additions. Open with the purpose (adding non-standard equipment, custom resistance elements), provide clear steps, explain K/Kf/Kv entry.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (adding custom resistance elements for non-standard equipment), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to step-by-step format**: Numbered list (open database editor → select General Resistance database → Add New → enter K/Kf/Kv data → save).
**Add "Required Data Fields" section**: Component name, resistance type (K, Kf, Kv), resistance value or polynomial input.
**Add "Resistance Types" section**: Explain K (loss coefficient), Kf (friction factor), Kv (flow coefficient) — when to use each.
**Add "Common Use Cases" section**: Inline filters, packed beds, cyclones, labyrinth seals, coils, tube bundles, expansion joints.
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify resistance appears in selection list, test in model.
**Add internal links**: P43 FluidFlow Database, P88 Hydraulic Components - General Resistances.
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
Glossary Links (first use only)
None
Internal Links
UP: P43 FluidFlow Database
SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles
DOWN: P88 Hydraulic Components - General Resistances
Selling Message
None
Grounding Tree Branches Used
General resistance database: K, Kf, Kv values
Specialized components: Inline filter, packed bed, cyclone, labyrinth seal, centrifuge, expansion joint, coils, tube bundles, constant head loss
Generic element: User-defined general resistance using polynomial input

MINI PRD: P79 — Adding New Pipe Roughness to Database
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P79 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Basic instructions for adding custom pipe roughness values to database. Minimal context on when custom roughness is needed, roughness impact. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Quick how-to guide for custom pipe roughness database additions. Open with the purpose (adding non-standard roughness for accurate friction loss calculations), provide clear steps, explain roughness impact on pressure drop.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (adding custom roughness for aged pipes, non-standard materials), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to step-by-step format**: Numbered list (open database editor → select Pipe Roughness database → Add New → enter roughness value → save).
**Add "Required Data Fields" section**: Material name, absolute roughness (mm or in), roughness range (min/max if applicable).
**Add "Roughness Impact" section**: How roughness affects friction loss (higher roughness → higher friction loss), link to pressure drop calculations.
**Add "When Custom Roughness Is Needed" section**: Aged pipes (increased roughness from scaling), exotic materials, field-measured roughness.
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify roughness appears in selection list, test impact in model (compare clean vs. aged pipe).
**Add internal links**: P43 FluidFlow Database, P70 Adding Pipe, P80 Pipe Scaling.
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
Glossary Links (first use only)
None
Internal Links
UP: P43 FluidFlow Database
SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles
Selling Message
None
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pipe roughness: Customizable for specific materials
Friction loss models: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach), Hazen Williams, Fixed Friction Factor, Shell-MIT
MINI PRD: P80 — Adding New Pipe Scaling Data to Database
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P80 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Basic instructions for adding custom pipe scaling data to database. Minimal context on when scaling is needed, scaling impact on flow capacity. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Quick how-to guide for custom pipe scaling database additions. Open with the purpose (modeling aged pipes with reduced internal diameter), provide clear steps, explain scaling impact on pressure drop and flow capacity.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (modeling pipe degradation from scaling, fouling, corrosion), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to step-by-step format**: Numbered list (open database editor → select Pipe Scaling database → Add New → enter scaling data → save).
**Add "Required Data Fields" section**: Scaling type (deposit thickness, diameter reduction %), material (calcium carbonate, iron oxide, etc.), thickness vs. time (if time-dependent).

**Add "Scaling Impact" section**: How scaling reduces effective diameter → increases friction loss → reduces flow capacity. Quantify with example (10% diameter reduction → ~40% pressure loss increase).
**Add "When Scaling Data Is Needed" section**: Aged water systems, high-hardness water, corrosive fluids, long-term performance modeling.
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify scaling data appears in selection list, test impact in model (compare new vs. scaled pipe).
**Add internal links**: P43 FluidFlow Database, P70 Adding Pipe, P79 Pipe Roughness.
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
Glossary Links (first use only)
None
Internal Links
UP: P43 FluidFlow Database
SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles
Selling Message
None
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pipe features: Pipe scaling can be applied (consider pipe degradation or current status)

**Add "Sizing Methods" section**: Economic velocity (Generaux equation), standard velocity (project-specific), pressure gradient (Pa/m or psi/100ft) — link to P16 Auto-Size Pipes.
**Add "When Custom Sizing Is Needed" section**: Project-specific design standards, client requirements, industry-specific rules (e.g., slurry minimum velocity to prevent settling).
**Add "Validation" section**: How to verify sizing criteria appears in auto-size tool, test in model.

SIDEWAYS: P70-P82 database addition articles

MINI PRD: P83 — Hydraulic Components - Boundaries
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P83 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now
Lists boundary types (Pressure, Flow, Tank/Vessel, Atmospheric) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for boundaries. Open with the purpose of boundaries (defining system inlet/outlet conditions), explain each boundary type with use cases, data requirements, and common mistakes.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of boundaries (defining system boundary conditions for calculations), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to component guide depth** (~1,500 words): Section for each boundary type (Pressure, Flow, Tank/Vessel, Atmospheric) with description, use cases, required data, common mistakes.

**Add "Pressure Boundary" section**: Fixed pressure inlet/outlet, use cases (pump suction from tank, discharge to vessel), required data (pressure, temperature), common mistakes (over-constraining with multiple pressures).

**Add "Flow Boundary" section**: Fixed flow rate, use cases (metered feed, specified demand), required data (flow rate, fluid properties), common mistakes (specifying flow at both ends → over-constrained).

**Add "Tank/Vessel Boundary" section**: Source or destination only, use cases (storage tanks, process vessels), required data (liquid level, vessel geometry), common mistakes (using as intermediate junction → should use tank element instead).
**Add "Atmospheric Boundary" section**: Sprinkler or customized discharge, use cases (fire protection, atmospheric vents), required data (elevation, K-factor for sprinklers).
**Add "Boundary Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of boundary types by application (pump suction, discharge to atmosphere, metered feed, etc.).
**Add "Common Mistakes" section**: Over-constraining (too many boundaries), under-constraining (no boundaries), mixing incompatible boundary types.
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (boundaries are fundamental concepts, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P07 How to Build a Model, P08 How to Enter Input Data, P59 Network Build Error (missing boundary troubleshooting)
DOWN: P84-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Boundaries: Pressure, Flow, Tank/Vessel Reservoir (source or destination only), Atmospheric Ends (Sprinkler or Customized discharge)
MINI PRD: P84 — Hydraulic Components - Boosters
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P84 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now
Lists booster types (centrifugal pumps, PD pumps, fans, blowers, compressors, turbines) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for boosters (pumps, fans, compressors). Open with the purpose of boosters (adding energy to fluid systems), explain each booster type with use cases, data requirements, and performance modeling.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of boosters (adding pressure/flow to systems), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~2,000 words): Section for each booster type (Centrifugal Pumps, Positive Displacement Pumps, Fans/Blowers, Compressors, Turbines) with description, use cases, data entry methods, performance modeling.

**Add "Centrifugal Pumps" section**: Description, use cases (most common liquid pumping), data entry (pump curve, manufacturer data, auto-size), performance modeling (duty point, NPSH, viscosity correction) — link to P14, P23, P29.

**Add "Positive Displacement Pumps" section**: Description, use cases (high-pressure, viscous fluids), data entry (flow vs. pressure rise), performance modeling (fixed displacement, speed control) — link to P27.

**Add "Fans/Blowers" section**: Description, use cases (low-pressure gas systems, ventilation, combustion air), data entry (fan curve, manufacturer data, auto-size), performance modeling (density correction, system effect) — link to P37.

**Add "Compressors" section**: Description, use cases (high-pressure gas systems, pneumatic transport), data entry (compressor curve, efficiency), performance modeling (adiabatic head, power consumption).
**Add "Turbines" section**: Description, use cases (energy recovery, power generation), data entry (turbine curve, efficiency), performance modeling (expansion, power output).
**Add "Booster Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of booster types by application (liquid low-pressure, liquid high-pressure, gas low-pressure, gas high-pressure).
**Add "Data Entry Methods" section**: Manual data entry vs. pump/fan curve database vs. auto-sizing — link to P71 Adding Boosters.
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in centrifugal pump section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in centrifugal pump section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in pump selection section
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P14 Modeling Centrifugal Pumps, P23 Auto-Size Pump, P27 Modeling PD Pumps, P29 Viscosity Correction, P37 Modeling Fans/Blowers, P71 Adding Boosters
DOWN: P85-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pumps: Centrifugal, Positive Displacement
Compressors/Blowers/Fans/Turbines: Auto-size based on flow or pressure rise
Pump curve database: Multiple vendors in default database
Auto-sizing: Industry standards for pumps and fans
Heat loss/gain: Customizable across all boosters
Slurry pump derating: Fixed Reduction, King, HI guidelines
MINI PRD: P85 — Hydraulic Components - Valves
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P85 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now
Lists valve types (butterfly, gate, globe, ball, etc.) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for valves. Open with the purpose of valves (throttling, isolation, flow control), explain each valve type with use cases, data requirements, and K-factor modeling.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of valves (throttling, isolation, flow control), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~2,000 words): Section for each valve type (Butterfly, Gate, Globe, Ball, Diaphragm, Plug, Needle, Y-globe, Slide, Penstock, 3-way, Fire Hydrant, Pinch) with description, use cases, K-factor modeling.
**Add "Valve Type Sections"**: Each valve type gets 1-2 paragraphs (description, typical applications, K-factor vs. opening %, when to use).
**Add "K-Factor Methods" section**: Idelchik, Miller, Crane, SAE — explain differences (Idelchik/Miller more accurate than Crane).
**Add "Valve Opening %" section**: How valve position affects pressure drop (0% closed → 100% open), customizable in infinite increments.
**Add "Valve Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of valve types by application (isolation, throttling, low-pressure drop, high-pressure drop, slurry service).
**Add "Data Entry Methods" section**: Default database vs. custom valve data — link to P73 Adding Valves.
**Add "Heat Loss/Gain" section**: Valves can model heat effects (rarely needed, but available).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).

Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P73 Adding Valves, P86 Controllers (control valves), P87 Check Valves
DOWN: P88-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Valve types: 14 types (Butterfly, Diaphragm, Ball, Gate, Globe, Ball float, Plug, Pinch, Y-globe, Needle, Slide, Penstock, 3-way, Fire hydrant)
K-factor methods: Idelchik, Miller, Crane, SAE
Valve database: Several models available, customizable
Valve opening: 0-100% in infinite increments
Heat loss/gain: Can be modeled
